claims.text: An unidentified object known as the Black Knight satellite has been orbiting Earth for about 13,000 years.

evidence.quote:
it's been
here for thirteen thousand years

claims.rationale: Central thesis. The 13,000-year figure is derived from Duncan Lunan's axial-precession adjustment to his 1973 star-map interpretation, which the host himself later notes 'doesn't hold up the scrutiny' and which Lunan publicly walked back.

claims.text: Nikola Tesla received artificial radio signals from space at his Colorado Springs lab in 1899.

evidence.quote:
at his lab in colorado springs nikola
tesla

tesla believed that this was evidence of
life on another planet

claims.rationale: Tesla did report receiving unusual periodic signals at Colorado Springs in 1899 and speculated publicly about extraterrestrial origin. The reception is well-documented historical record; the interpretation as aliens is what the host later says was actually pulsars.

claims.text: Tesla's and Marconi's mysterious radio signals were actually pulsars, which weren't officially discovered until 1968.

evidence.quote:
they weren't
aliens they were pulsars and pulsars
weren't discovered officially until 1968

claims.rationale: Jocelyn Bell Burnell's 1967 pulsar discovery (published 1968) is well-established astronomy; pulsars produce regular radio bursts that could be mistaken for intelligent signals. The retroactive identification of Tesla's specific signals as pulsar emissions is plausible but not formally demonstrated.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Provides the natural-phenomenon explanation for what Tesla interpreted as artificial signals.

claims.text: On May 14, 1954 American newspapers reported that Major Donald Keyhoe claimed one or two artificial satellites were circling Earth.

evidence.quote:
on may 14 1954 three years before
sputnik

he claimed that u.s government
scientists working out of white sands

claims.rationale: The 1954 Keyhoe report is real and his subsequent NICAP work is documented. The 'artificial satellites' claim was Keyhoe's interpretation of contested data; the U.S. government never confirmed satellites three years before Sputnik.

claims.text: The U.S. Defense Department acknowledged an unidentified silent satellite in a near-polar orbit on February 11, 1960.

evidence.quote:
on
february 11 1960 the new york times ran
an article

claims.rationale: The February 1960 NYT article is real and described an unidentified tracked object. Host later concedes the object was identified later as the off-course Discoverer V capsule.

claims.text: The 1960 black knight object was actually the off-course Discoverer 5 capsule, though Discoverer itself was a cover story for the Corona spy-satellite program.

evidence.quote:
the us government said this was
actually the discoverer 5 capsule that
got off course

claims.rationale: Discoverer/Corona was a real classified spy-satellite program; Discoverer V malfunction of August 1959 is documented. Host is skeptical because of date discrepancies but the declassified Corona history supports the Discoverer identification.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Gives the mundane origin for the 1960 tracked object.

claims.text: The black knight satellite was moving in retrograde orbit, opposite Earth's rotation, a capability unavailable to 1950s Earth-launched satellites.

evidence.quote:
it couldn't be sputnik moves in
pro-grade orbit

the object he saw was moving in
retrograde orbit

claims.rationale: Retrograde orbit is physically possible but requires more energy; it was uncommon in the 1950s. The eyewitness was a single Venezuelan researcher observing Sputnik 2; the claim is not independently verified by tracking data.

claims.text: Jacques Vallee filmed the black knight satellite from a Paris laboratory, but the materials were immediately confiscated.

evidence.quote:
jacques valet observed tracked and even
filmed the black knight satellite

the materials were immediately
confiscated

claims.rationale: Jacques Vallee is a real, well-known UFO researcher and his claim of tracking an unidentified object from a Paris observatory appears in his memoirs. The object's identity as the black knight and the destruction of the data are unverifiable.

claims.text: Astronaut Gordon Cooper, while piloting Mercury 9, saw a green glowing object that NASA officially attributed to carbon-dioxide-induced hallucinations.

evidence.quote:
piloted mercury 9 which was the longest
american space mission

cooper reported seeing a green
glowing object

claims.rationale: Cooper's Faith 7 / Mercury-Atlas 9 flight (May 1963) is fact; Cooper did later discuss UFO sightings publicly. The specific Mercury 9 green-object account exists but NASA's CO2/electronic-malfunction attribution is the documented official stance.

claims.text: Duncan Lunan claimed in 1973 that long-delayed radio echoes from the black knight decoded into a star map pointing to Epsilon Boötis.

evidence.quote:
duncan london said he had
identified a hidden radio message sent
by the black knight satellite

claims.rationale: Duncan Lunan's 1973 Spaceflight article on LDE star maps is real and citable. The claim is about what Lunan published, not whether the decoding is correct.

claims.text: Duncan Lunan's star-map decoding of the LDE signals does not hold up to scrutiny and he publicly walked back the scientific-evidence claim.

evidence.quote:
duncan london's translation of
the lds into star maps
it doesn't hold up the scrutiny he later
walked the claims back

claims.rationale: Lunan's partial retraction is documented in the LDE literature. His methodology — reversing axes until a pattern emerged — is textbook p-hacking.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] The author's own retraction undercuts the star-map decoding.
[undercuts] The 13,000-year age estimate depends on Lunan's precession-adjusted star map.

claims.text: The 1998 STS-88 photos showing the black knight are actually a thermal cover lost during an astronaut spacewalk.

evidence.quote:
the official explanation is that during
a spacewalk an astronaut was installing
a thermal cover

claims.rationale: The lost-thermal-blanket explanation is documented in STS-88 mission transcripts; the object is catalogued orbital debris that re-entered shortly after.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] The most widely circulated photographic evidence for the black knight is explained as ordinary space debris.

claims.text: The black knight is a technology left by our ancestors containing DNA of a human-like species as a catastrophe safeguard.

evidence.quote:
piece of technology left by our
ancestors thousands or millions of years
ago

that arc contains dna information of
human-like species from long ago

claims.rationale: Pure speculation with no physical evidence. Host offers as one possible interpretation, hedged with 'some call it' and 'could'. Near-zero directTruth because it both requires a lost prehuman civilization with orbital technology and invokes a DNA-ark concept with no basis.

claims.text: The modern black-knight legend is a composite of several distinct stories, each with a mundane explanation.

evidence.quote:
the modern version of
the legend is a combination of a few
stories

claims.rationale: This composite-myth framing is the host's own closing verdict and matches the scholarly skeptic consensus on the black knight legend (see Brian Dunning's Skeptoid treatment and the Wikipedia conspiracy-theory article). Strong directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] If the legend is a composite of independently-explained stories, the unified 13,000-year satellite narrative falls apart.
